constitution:
- Organize Refine as Foundation, Capabilities, and Surfaces. Foundation holds the durable concepts, Capabilities own the powers and product semantics, and Surfaces remain replaceable adapters.
- Keep the foundation small and legible: Nodes, Goals, Features, target-app context, workflow state, settings, and evidence must remain explicit enough for people and agents to inspect.
- Keep Process, Agents, and Workflow as shared capabilities. They may evolve or be replaced, but their responsibilities and composition must remain coherent across the system.
- Preserve local ownership. Authoritative state, Git history, settings, runtime records, and agent output must be inspectable and recoverable; caches and projections must remain derived.
- Be agent-first by using installed coding-agent CLIs and host tools through shared configuration, process supervision, workflow, and evidence rather than provider- or surface-specific product paths.
- Treat workflow as durable state advancement, process as the observable execution substrate, and agents as actors that use both without redefining either.
- Prefer mitigation and accountability over broad capability denial: isolation, Git, governance, quality, review, visible errors, and recovery should make powerful work safe enough to use.
- Preserve room for architectural discovery. A better design may replace current machinery when it explains the tradeoff, protects product intent, and supplies proportionate migration and verification evidence.
product: Refine is a local-first, agent-first operating layer for software delivery. It turns understandable Goals and Features into reviewable changes by coordinating explicit workflow state, supervised processes, installed coding-agent CLIs, Git isolation, quality, governance, and durable evidence across one or many Nodes. Its core promise is that powerful autonomous work remains user-owned, inspectable, recoverable, and consistent across replaceable human and agent surfaces.

rules.text: Respect the Foundation-Capabilities-Surfaces boundary: durable concepts belong in the model and target-app foundation, product behavior and orchestration belong in shared capabilities, and UI, CLI, API, desktop, and agent surfaces stay thin and semantically consistent.

Compose the existing Process, Agents, Workflow, Git, state, quality, governance, and evidence capabilities before creating new machinery. Each concern should have one authoritative implementation and source of truth unless the Goal explicitly proposes an architectural replacement.

Keep authoritative target-app state local, durable, inspectable, and outside the primary source worktree; keep runtime artifacts separate and projections or caches rebuildable. State-layout changes require a compatible migration or explicit transition plan.

Keep the daemon and surfaces responsive by running agent, Git, quality, lifecycle, and other long-running work through the supervised process substrate with explicit ownership, status, logs, cancellation, failure feedback, and recovery.

Coordinate parallel or distributed work explicitly through Node ownership, workflow claims and capacity, dependency or Feature ordering, and isolated Git branches or worktrees so concurrent changes remain reviewable and recoverable.

Require evidence proportional to actual architectural and operational risk. Verify the affected behavior with the smallest focused checks that provide confidence, including integration or failure-path evidence when a change alters concurrency, persistence, process, Git, release, recovery, or cross-surface semantics. Update only affected docs/intent pages when documented product intent or an architectural contract changes; do not require unrelated documentation or broad cleanup.

Allow architectural evolution when a Goal states the protected product intent, explains why the new design is better, updates capability boundaries and intent docs, and provides migration, compatibility, and verification evidence proportionate to the change.
